data.body:
PASS-1 review (G6 — approach soundness, anti-patterns & alternative-selection) for the docs-only plan "Docs: gate behavior, config surface, fail-open, cert semantics, CI pattern" (cross-referenced here as the Pass-4 coach doc target).

VERDICT: No G6 findings — clean.

Reasoning (grounded):
- MECHANISM CORRECTNESS: The mechanism is "author docs covering behavior / config (incl. the disable kill-switch) / fail-open / cert semantics / CI pattern, cross-referencing 6f2d WS-DOC." For a docs deliverable the load-bearing correctness risk is honesty/completeness, and AC#4 ("States end-to-end time-to-first-work honestly (not just the 50ms claim check)") explicitly forecloses the misleading-latency anti-pattern flagged in 5fd2's review (presenting the ~50ms fast-claim number as user-facing time-to-first-work while heavy review runs out-of-band). Sound.
- FITNESS-FOR-PURPOSE: solves the named problem ("the gate serves a broad, diverse client base; it must be honestly documented") rather than a proxy — the ACs map 1:1 to the topics requiring honest documentation, including the Pass-4 affirmative coach (move registry + how coaching is delivered + advisory-only), consistent with 75a9's advisory/single-turn/reference-by-id design.
- APPROACH SELECTION: the only structurally-different choice for docs-only work is duplicate-the-workflow-engine-content vs cross-reference. The plan chose cross-reference ("the canonical workflow-engine intended-use doc lives in 6f2d"), the correct call — it avoids a divergence/duplication anti-pattern and respects 6f2d WS-DOC as single source of truth. Defensible; generated alternatives discarded (not written into the plan).
- POSITIVE RATIONALE: present ("must be honestly documented" for a broad client base).

ANTI-FP: documentation authoring is a well-understood activity with no genuine structurally-different "better approach" to coach beyond the duplicate-vs-reference question the plan already handles. G6's alternative-generation requirement is effectively not-applicable. Clean for G6.
